elements.sent-2411-001: In this article we study the spectral, linear and nonlinear stability of stationary shock profile solutions to the Lax-Wendroff scheme for hyperbolic conservation laws.
elements.sent-2411-002: We first clarify the spectral stability of such solutions depending on the convexity of the flux for the underlying conservation law.
elements.sent-2411-003: The main contribution of this article is a detailed study of the so-called Green's function for the linearized numerical scheme.
elements.sent-2411-004: As evidenced on numerical simulations, the Green's function exhibits a highly oscillating behavior ahead of the leading wave before this wave reaches the shock location.
elements.sent-2411-005: One of our main results gives a quantitative description of this behavior.
elements.sent-2411-006: Because of the existence of a one-parameter family of stationary shock profiles, the linearized numerical scheme admits the eigenvalue $1$ that is embedded in its continuous spectrum, which gives rise to several contributions in the Green's function.
elements.sent-2411-007: Our detailed analysis of the Green's function describes these contributions by means of a so-called activation function.
elements.sent-2411-008: For large times, the activation function describes how the mass of the initial condition accumulates along the eigenvector associated with the eigenvalue $1$ of the linearized numerical scheme.
elements.sent-2411-009: We can then obtain sharp decay estimates for the linearized numerical scheme in polynomially weighted spaces, which in turn yield a nonlinear orbital stability result for spectrally stable stationary shock profiles.
elements.sent-2411-010: This nonlinear result is obtained despite the lack of uniform $\ell^1$ estimates for the Green's function of the linearized numerical scheme, the lack of such estimates being linked with the dispersive nature of the numerical scheme.
elements.sent-2411-011: This dispersive feature is in sharp contrast with previous results on the orbital stability of traveling waves or discrete shock profiles for parabolic perturbations of conservation laws.

elements.sent-2411-013: Throughout this article, we let $\N^*$ denote the set of positive integers $\{ 1,2,3,\dots \}$ and $\N$ denote the set of integers (including $0$).
elements.sent-2411-014: We let $\R$ denote the set of real numbers, $\R^+:=[0,+\infty)$ the set of nonnegative real numbers and $\R^{+* :=(0,+\infty)$ the set of positive numbers.}
elements.sent-2411-015: We also let $\C$ denote the set of complex numbers.
elements.sent-2411-017: If $w$ is a complex number, the notation $B_r(w)$ stands for the (round) open ball in $\C$ centered at $w$ and with radius $r>0$, that is $B_r(w) := \{ z \in \C \, | \, |z-w|<r \}$.
elements.sent-2411-018: We shall also use ``square balls'' in the complex plane.
elements.sent-2411-019: Namely, for any $r>0$, the notation $\mathbf{B _r(w)$ stands for the open set: \begin{equation}B_r(w) \, := \Big\{ z \in \C \, \big| \, \max \big( |\rm Re (z-w)|,|\rm Im (z-w)| \big)<r \Big\} \, .\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-021: For any positive number $r$, we let $r \, \cercle$ denote the circle in $\C$ centered at the origin and with radius $r$.
elements.sent-2411-022: We also let ${\mathscr M _{n,k (\C)$ denote the set of $n \times k$ matrices with complex entries.}}
elements.sent-2411-024: For $q \in [1,+\infty)$, we let $\ell^q(\Z;\C)$ denote the space of complex valued sequences $\mathbf{v =(v_j)_{j\in \Z}$ indexed by $\Z$ and such that the quantity: \begin{equation}\sum_{j \in \Z} \, |v_j|^q\end{equation} is finite.}
elements.sent-2411-025: The $1/q$-th power of this quantity defines a norm with which $\ell^q(\Z;\C)$ becomes a Banach space.
elements.sent-2411-027: For $q=+\infty$, we let $\ell^\infty(\Z;\C)$ denote the space of bounded complex valued sequences indexed by $\Z$.
elements.sent-2411-028: This space is equipped with the norm: \begin{equation}\sup_{j \in \Z} \, |v_j| \, ,\end{equation} which we shall refer to as $\| \cdot \|_{\ell^\infty}$.
elements.sent-2411-029: When equipped with this norm, the space $\ell^\infty(\Z;\C)$ is a Banach algebra (the product between two sequences being here understood in the pointwise sense $(v \, w)_j :=v_j \, w_j$ for each $j \in \Z$).
elements.sent-2411-030: Sequences will be denoted with bold letters, while their evaluation at a given integer will be denoted with standard letters.
elements.sent-2411-032: A typical example is the inequality: \begin{equation}\forall \, x \in \R^+ \, ,\quad x \, \exp(-c \, x) \, \le C \, \exp(-c \, x) \, ,\end{equation} where, of course, the constant $c$ on the right-hand side of the inequality is not the same as in the left-hand side.
elements.sent-2411-033: The dependance of the constants on the various involved parameters is made precise throughout the article.
elements.sent-2411-034: In order to avoid overloading some expressions, we sometimes write: \begin{equation}\sum_{m=0}^x\end{equation} for a sum that runs over the integer $m$ from $m=0$ to $m=x$, even when $x$ is a positive real number that is not an integer.
elements.sent-2411-035: In that case, it is understood that the sum runs up to the largest number that is less than $x$.
elements.sent-2411-036: This will allow us to avoir using the integer part of several quantities.
elements.sent-2411-037: This article is devoted to a detailed stability analysis of stationary shock profiles for the Lax-Wendroff scheme.
elements.sent-2411-038: The Lax-Wendroff scheme is a finite difference approximation of hyperbolic conservation laws that is formally second order accurate (at least for smooth solutions), see \cite{Kroner,LeVeque .}
elements.sent-2411-039: As any second order accurate numerical scheme, the Lax-Wendroff scheme gives rise to spurious oscillations once the solution to the conservation law develops discontinuities, that is, once shock waves have appeared.
elements.sent-2411-040: This is evidenced for instance in the numerical simulation reported in Figure \ref{fig:simulation-num-1 below.}
elements.sent-2411-041: This undesirable numerical feature is usually corrected by introducing flux limiters or other more involved numerical treatments (essentially non-oscillatory schemes, weighted non-oscillatory schemes and so on).
elements.sent-2411-042: Our goal here is to show that despite the formation of oscillatory wave trains in the numerical computation of shock waves, the Lax-Wendroff scheme gives rise to stable stationary shock profiles for convex or concave fluxes, and for even other situations.
elements.sent-2411-043: The whole point is to clarify the meaning of ``stable'' in the previous sentence.
elements.sent-2411-044: Following a long line of research, we aim at studying this stability problem by first showing a spectral stability result, then turning this spectral stability result into a linear stability result by proving sharp decay estimates for the linearized numerical scheme.
elements.sent-2411-045: Obtaining these sharp linear decay estimates is the cornerstone of our work.
elements.sent-2411-046: The final step of the analysis is to use the linear decay estimates in order to obtain an orbital stability result for the nonlinear dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-047: The approach is definitely not new.
elements.sent-2411-048: For the Lax-Wendroff scheme, it was followed by Smyrlis \cite{Smyrlis who dealt with the exact same problem as the one we are looking at here.}
elements.sent-2411-049: However, the functional framework that we use is much larger than the one in \cite{Smyrlis where the introduction of exponential weights stabilizes the linearized operator.}
elements.sent-2411-050: Actually, the introduction of exponential weights gives rise to a spectral gap for some carefully chosen parameters, thus bypassing a detailed stability analysis that would take the flux properties into account.
elements.sent-2411-051: We consider here a larger and, probably, more natural functional framework in which the spectrum of the linearized operator depends on the flux of the conservation law.
elements.sent-2411-052: The overall strategy is the same as the one followed in the contributions \cite{godillon,Coeuret1,Coeuret2 .}
elements.sent-2411-053: We start from a given discrete shock profile, which is quite easy for the Lax-Wendroff scheme since this numerical scheme exhibits exact, piecewise constant, stationary discrete shock profiles that are the mere projections on the numerical grid of the continuous step shock.
elements.sent-2411-054: We linearize the Lax-Wendroff scheme around this stationary solution and try to locate the spectrum of the linearized evolution operator.
elements.sent-2411-055: Enlarging the functional framework has some major impacts, the first of which being that we have to take into account the (neutral) eigenvalue $1$ for the linearized numerical scheme.
elements.sent-2411-056: This eigenvalue arises by ``translation invariance'', as explained in \cite{Smyrlis (see also \cite{Serre-notes for a general overview on discrete shock profiles).}}
elements.sent-2411-057: Translation invariance means here that there exists a one-parameter smooth family of stationary discrete shock profiles\footnote{For continuous problems, such as parabolic perturbations of conservation laws, the one-parameter family of profiles is obtained by simply translating in space a given profile, but the construction of shock profiles is unfortunately more complicated for discrete problems.. .}
elements.sent-2411-058: The rest of the spectrum is located by analyzing the so-called Evans function associated with the shock profile.
elements.sent-2411-059: The Evans function plays the role of a characteristic polynomial for the linearized operator.
elements.sent-2411-060: The general construction of the Evans function is detailed in \cite{Serre-notes , see also \cite{godillon,Coeuret1 .}}
elements.sent-2411-061: In our case, the construction is far easier because our reference discrete shock is piecewise constant so the Evans function reduces to what could be referred to as a Lopatinskii determinant (by analogy with the stability analysis of shock waves \cite{benzoni-serre ).}
elements.sent-2411-062: Our problem is similar to the stability analysis of the discrete shock profiles for the Godunov scheme \cite{BGS in which the shock profiles are also piecewise constant.}
elements.sent-2411-063: For scalar equations, as we consider here, the expression of the Lopatinskii determinant is simple enough so that we can analyze the location of its zeroes in the case where the flux of the conservation law is either convex or concave.
elements.sent-2411-064: Namely, we shall show that for convex or concave fluxes, the Lopatinskii determinant has only $1$ as a (simple) zero in the region $\Ubar$ of the complex plane.
elements.sent-2411-065: This corresponds, in the terminology of \cite{Serre-notes to a spectrally stable situation.}
elements.sent-2411-066: Our analysis encompasses a particular (symmetric) case that was considered in the seminal work \cite{HHL .}
elements.sent-2411-067: Once we have located the spectrum of the operator, we can construct the so-called spatial Green's function, that is the fundamental solution of the resolvent equation.
elements.sent-2411-068: For spectrally stable configurations, we show that the spatial Green's function has a meromorphic extension near $1$ and it can also be holomorphically extended near any other point of the unit circle.
elements.sent-2411-069: This is a key step towards the decomposition and the proof of sharp decay bounds for the Green's function of the linearized evolution operator.
elements.sent-2411-070: In the parabolic case, the Green's function satisfies uniform (in time) $\ell^1$ estimates (in space) and it also satisfies decaying (in time) $\ell^\infty$ estimates (in space), just like the heat kernel.
elements.sent-2411-071: For the Lax-Wendroff scheme, there is unfortunately no hope to obtain such favorable uniform bounds.
elements.sent-2411-072: Indeed, it is already known that when applied to the transport equation, the Green's function of the Lax-Wendroff scheme does not enjoy uniform $\ell^1$ estimates.
elements.sent-2411-073: This failure of $\ell^1$ stability, that is linked to the dispersive behavior of the Lax-Wendroff scheme, has been identified in a general context by Thom\'ee \cite{Thomee for convolution operators.}
elements.sent-2411-074: The analysis of $\ell^1$ instability was later refined in \cite{hedstrom1,hedstrom2 in order to make the instability growth rates percise.}
elements.sent-2411-075: Rather than following \cite{hedstrom1,hedstrom2 for the Lax-Wendroff scheme, we shall build here on the recent work \cite{jfcAMBP by one of the authors.}}
elements.sent-2411-076: The analysis in \cite{jfcAMBP gives a precise description of the Green's function for this numerical scheme in the context of the Cauchy problem for the transport equation\footnote{The quantitative estimates in \cite{jfcAMBP are more accurate in many regimes than the ones in \cite{hedstrom1,hedstrom2 and this is crucial for several arguments that we use below.. .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-077: We extend here the analysis of \cite{jfcAMBP to the context of the shock profile stability analysis in which Fourier analysis is no longer available due to spatial variations.}
elements.sent-2411-078: We therefore substitute the so-called spatial dynamics approach in place of Fourier analysis and use the inverse Laplace transform to obtain a representation formula for the Green's function of the linearized operator.
elements.sent-2411-079: The main difficulty that we shall face arises from the singularity of the spatial Green's function at the eigenvalue $1$ (that is imbedded in the continuous spectrum).
elements.sent-2411-080: This pole gives rise to a leading contribution in the Green's function which, following \cite{Coeuret2 , we refer to as an activation function.}
elements.sent-2411-081: This function can be thought of as a primitive of the Green's function for the Cauchy problem.
elements.sent-2411-082: Detailed bounds on this activation function are given in Appendix \ref{appendixA at the end of this article.}
elements.sent-2411-083: In particular, a lengthy -though crucial- argument here is to obtain a uniform bound for the activation function.
elements.sent-2411-084: This is rather trivial in the parabolic setting thanks to uniform $\ell^1$ estimates for the Green's function but such uniform $\ell^1$ estimates are known to fail in the dispersive setting.
elements.sent-2411-085: Our uniform bound for the activation function is reminiscent of the main result in \cite{ELR where such a uniform bound is also the cornerstone of the argument.}
elements.sent-2411-086: Once we have an accurate description of the Green's function with sharp bounds for each term in its decomposition, the nonlinear orbital stability result follows more or less by using standard tools as in \cite{BHR .}
elements.sent-2411-087: Since our Green's function does not satisfy uniform $\ell^1$ estimates and exhibits a dispersive instability\footnote{The $\ell^\infty$ decay of the Green's function for the Lax-Wendroff scheme is also slower than for the parabolic situation that is considered in \cite{BHR .}}
elements.sent-2411-088: We need to work in polynomially weighted $\ell^p$ spaces and carefully play with the weights in the definition of the norms in order to recover some integrability in time, which is a crucial feature of bootstrap arguments.
elements.sent-2411-089: This is less favorable than the situation for semi-discrete shocks that is dealt with in \cite{BHR where the time translation invariance also allows to use a larger functional framework.}
elements.sent-2411-090: The fully discrete situation does not give as many tools to deal with the nonlinear argument.
elements.sent-2411-091: The plan of this article is the following.
elements.sent-2411-092: In Chapter \ref{chapter2 we introduce the numerical scheme, we give the expression of the reference shock profile that we consider and state our main results.}
elements.sent-2411-093: For the sake of clarity, we have split the main results in four theorems, the first two being devoted to spectral stability, the third one being devoted to linear stability, and the fourth and main one being devoted to nonlinear orbital stability.
elements.sent-2411-094: We also report on some numerical simulations that illustrate these results.
elements.sent-2411-095: Chapter \ref{chapter3 is devoted to the spectral analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-096: The linear decay estimates are proved in Chapter \ref{chapter4 with the help of several key estimates that are given in Appendix \ref{appendixA .}}
elements.sent-2411-097: Eventually, the nonlinear analysis is detailed in Chapter \ref{chapter5 .}
elements.chapter-2411-003: The Lax-Wendroff scheme. Notation and main results
elements.section-2411-001: The Lax-Wendroff scheme and its stationary shock profiles
elements.sent-2411-098: We consider in this article a scalar conservation law of the form: \begin{equation}\label{law} \partial_t u + \partial_x f(u) = 0, \quad x\in\R,\end{equation} with a smooth flux $f \in \mathscr{C ^\infty(\R;\R)$.}
elements.sent-2411-099: Since the solutions to the Cauchy problem for \eqref{law generically develop singularities in finite time \cite{Dafermos,Serre1 , we directly consider piecewise smooth solutions to \eqref{law , which we assume to be stationary for simplicity: \begin{equation}\label{shock} u(t,x) = \left\{ \begin{array}{ll} u_\ell, x<0, u_r, x>0. \end{array} \right.\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-100: For \eqref{shock to be a weak solution to \eqref{law , the so-called Rankine-Hugoniot relation must hold: \begin{equation}\label{RH} f(u_\ell) = f(u_r) \, ,\end{equation} and we also enforce the so-called entropy criterion \cite{Serre1 : \begin{equation}\label{entropy} f'(u_r) < 0 < f'(u_\ell) \, .\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-101: The Lax entropy inequalities \eqref{entropy imply that the characteristics stemming from either side of the shock wave (that is located here at $x=0$) enter the shock in positive time, as depicted in Figure \ref{fig:characteristics .}}

elements.sent-2411-102: Our goal here is to understand the influence of a high order discretization procedure on the stability of the shock \eqref{shock and more specifically whether dispersion in a numerical scheme may rule out linear or nonlinear stability.}
elements.sent-2411-103: Let us recall that a rather complete existence and stability theory for monotone schemes has been developed in \cite{Jennings but monotone schemes are at most first order accurate \cite{Kroner,LeVeque .}}
elements.sent-2411-104: We therefore pursue the analysis of \cite{Smyrlis and try to develop a rather complete stability analysis of discrete shock profiles for the Lax-Wendroff scheme.}
elements.sent-2411-105: This is a model situation for a high order finite difference scheme and we hope that some of our arguments below may prove useful for other discretization procedures of conservation laws.
elements.sent-2411-106: We therefore introduce a space step $\Delta x>0$ and a time step $\Delta t>0$ that are always chosen such that the ratio $\lambda:=\Delta t/\Delta x$ is kept constant.
elements.sent-2411-107: For any couple of integers $n \in \N$ and $j \in \Z$, the solution to \eqref{law is approximated, on the time-space cell $[n \, \Delta t, (n+1) \, \Delta t) \times [j \, \Delta x,(j+1) \, \Delta x)$ by a constant value $u_j^n$, that is iteratively defined with respect to $n$ according to the formula: \begin{equation}\label{schemeLW} \forall \, (n,j) \in \N \times \Z \, ,\quad u_j^{n+1} = u_j^n - \lambda \left( \cF_\lambda(u_j^n,u_{j+1}^n)-\cF_\lambda(u_{j-1}^n,u_{j}^n) \right),\end{equation} with a numerical flux $\cF_\lambda$ that is defined by: \begin{equation}\label{fluxLW} \forall \, (u,v) \in \R^2 \, ,\quad \cF_\lambda(u,v) := \dfrac{1}{2}\left(f(u)+f(v)\right) - \dfrac{\lambda}{2}f'\left(\frac{u+v}{2}\right)\left(f(v)-f(u)\right).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-108: The numerical scheme \eqref{schemeLW -\eqref{fluxLW , that is referred to as the Lax-Wendroff scheme and dates back to \cite{LaxWendroff , is a formally second order accurate approximation procedure for \eqref{law , see \cite{Kroner,LeVeque .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-109: The initial condition $(u_j^0)_{j \in \Z}$ for \eqref{schemeLW will be chosen as a small perturbation of a discretized version of the shock wave \eqref{shock .}}

elements.sent-2411-111: Namely, if we consider the weak solution \eqref{shock to \eqref{law , then the following sequence: \begin{equation}\label{DSP} \overline{u}_j^n:=\left\{ \begin{array}{ll} u_\ell, j \le 0, u_r, j \ge 1, \end{array} \right. \quad n \in \N \, ,\end{equation} defines a stationary solution to \eqref{schemeLW -\eqref{fluxLW since it does not depend on $n$ and satisfies (we omit the index $n$ in $\overline{u _j$ from now on): \begin{equation}\forall \, j \in \Z \, ,\quad \cF_\lambda(\overline{u}_j,\overline{u}_{j+1}) = \cF_\lambda(\overline{u}_{j-1},\overline{u}_j) = f(u_\ell) = f(u_r) \, ,\end{equation} the final equality coming from the Rankine-Hugoniot relation \eqref{RH .}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-306: For $\alpha_m=\alpha_r$, we compute: \begin{equation}D(\alpha_r,Z)= \, \alpha_\ell \, |\alpha_r| +\alpha_r^2 +\sqrt{Z^2 +2 \, \alpha_\ell^2 \, Z+\alpha_\ell^2} +\sqrt{Z^2 +2 \, \alpha_r^2 \, Z+\alpha_r^2} +(Z+\alpha_r^2) \, \dfrac{Z+\alpha_\ell^2}{\sqrt{Z^2 +2 \, \alpha_\ell^2 \, Z+\alpha_\ell^2}} +(Z-\alpha_\ell \, |\alpha_r|) \, \dfrac{Z+\alpha_r^2}{\sqrt{Z^2 +2 \, \alpha_r^2 \, Z+\alpha_r^2}} \ge \, \alpha_\ell \, |\alpha_r| \underbrace{\left( 1 -\dfrac{Z+\alpha_r^2}{\sqrt{Z^2 +2 \, \alpha_r^2 \, Z+\alpha_r^2}} \right)}_{\ge 0} +\sqrt{Z^2 +2 \, \alpha_\ell^2 \, Z+\alpha_\ell^2} +\sqrt{Z^2 +2 \, \alpha_r^2 \, Z+\alpha_r^2} \, .\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-072: \begin{equation}D(\alpha_r,Z)= \, \alpha_\ell \, |\alpha_r| +\alpha_r^2 +\sqrt{Z^2 +2 \, \alpha_\ell^2 \, Z+\alpha_\ell^2} +\sqrt{Z^2 +2 \, \alpha_r^2 \, Z+\alpha_r^2} +(Z+\alpha_r^2) \, \dfrac{Z+\alpha_\ell^2}{\sqrt{Z^2 +2 \, \alpha_\ell^2 \, Z+\alpha_\ell^2}} +(Z-\alpha_\ell \, |\alpha_r|) \, \dfrac{Z+\alpha_r^2}{\sqrt{Z^2 +2 \, \alpha_r^2 \, Z+\alpha_r^2}} \ge \, \alpha_\ell \, |\alpha_r| \underbrace{\left( 1 -\dfrac{Z+\alpha_r^2}{\sqrt{Z^2 +2 \, \alpha_r^2 \, Z+\alpha_r^2}} \right)}_{\ge 0} +\sqrt{Z^2 +2 \, \alpha_\ell^2 \, Z+\alpha_\ell^2} +\sqrt{Z^2 +2 \, \alpha_r^2 \, Z+\alpha_r^2} \, .\end{equation}

elements.sent-2411-310: For $\alpha_m=\alpha_r$ and $Z \le -2 \, \alpha^2$, we have: \begin{equation}D(\alpha_r,Z) = \, Z^2+\alpha_r^2 \, Z +\alpha_\ell \, \alpha_r \, Z +(|Z|-\alpha_r^2) \, \sqrt{Z^2 +2 \, \alpha_\ell^2 \, Z+\alpha_\ell^2}+(|Z|+\alpha_\ell \, |\alpha_r|) \, \sqrt{Z^2 +2 \, \alpha_r^2 \, Z+\alpha_r^2} +\sqrt{Z^2 +2 \, \alpha_\ell^2 \, Z+\alpha_\ell^2} \, \sqrt{Z^2 +2 \, \alpha_r^2 \, Z+\alpha_r^2} -\alpha_\ell \, |\alpha_r| \, .\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-385: As a consequence, summarizing the above results, we have obtained for $j_0\leq-1$ that \begin{equation}\label{prop1-derniercas} G^{j_0}_j(z)=\left\{ \begin{array}{ll} \kappa_r(z)^{j-2} \, \kappa_\ell^u(z)^{-j_0-1} \left[{\rm e}_1^t \, \B(z)^{-1}\M_{2,1}(z)\M_{1,0}(z)\Pi_\ell^{u}(z) \A_\ell {\rm e}_2 \right] E_r^s(z), j \geq 2, \kappa_\ell(z)^{j} \, \kappa_\ell^u(z)^{-j_0-1} \, \left[{\rm e}_2^t \, \B(z)^{-1}\M_{2,1}(z)\M_{1,0}(z)\Pi_\ell^{u}(z)\A_\ell {\rm e}_2 \right] E_\ell^s(z) \qquad +\kappa_\ell^u(z)^{j-j_0-1}\Pi_\ell^{u}(z)\A_\ell {\rm e}_2, j_0< j \leq 0, \kappa_\ell(z)^{j} \, \kappa_\ell^u(z)^{-j_0-1} \, \left[ {\rm e}_2^t \, \B(z)^{-1}\M_{2,1}(z)\M_{1,0}(z)\Pi_\ell^{u}(z) \A_\ell {\rm e}_2 \right] E_\ell^s(z) \qquad -\kappa_\ell(z)^{j-j_0-1} \, \Pi_\ell^{s}(z)\A_\ell {\rm e}_2, j\leq j_0. \end{array} \right.\end{equation}.}

elements.sent-2411-386: For later use, we introduce the notation: \begin{equation}\M_{2,1}(z)\M_{1,0}(z) = \begin{pmatrix} m_1(z) m_2(z) m_3(z) m_4(z) \end{pmatrix},\end{equation} We then compute: \bqs {\rm e _2^t \, \Pi_\ell^{s (z)\A_\ell {\rm e _2 = \dfrac{2 \, \kappa_\ell(z) {\alpha_\ell(1-\alpha_\ell)(\kappa_\ell(z)-\kappa_\ell^{u (z)) , \quad {\rm e _2^t \, \Pi_\ell^{u (z)\A_\ell {\rm e _2 = \dfrac{2 \, \kappa_\ell^u(z) {\alpha_\ell(1-\alpha_\ell)(\kappa_\ell^{u (z)-\kappa_\ell(z)) , \eqs as well as \begin{equation}{\rm e}_1^t \, \B(z)^{-1}\M_{2,1}(z)\M_{1,0}(z)\Pi_\ell^{u}(z) \A_\ell {\rm e}_2 = \dfrac{2 \, \Big( b_2(z)(m_1(z)+\kappa_\ell^{u}(z)m_2(z))-b_1(z)(m_3(z)+\kappa_\ell^{u}(z)m_4(z)) \Big)}{\alpha_\ell(1-\alpha_\ell) \Delta(z) (\kappa_\ell^{u}(z)-\kappa_\ell(z))}, {\rm e}_2^t \, \B(z)^{-1}\M_{2,1}(z)\M_{1,0}(z)\Pi_\ell^{u}(z) \A_\ell {\rm e}_2 = \dfrac{2 \, \Big( -\kappa_r(z)(m_1(z)+\kappa_\ell^{u}(z)m_2(z))+m_3(z)+\kappa_\ell^{u}(z)m_4(z) \Big)}{\alpha_\ell(1-\alpha_\ell) \Delta(z) (\kappa_\ell^{u}(z)-\kappa_\ell(z))}.\end{equation}.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-387: Looking at either the first or second coordinate of the vector $G^{j_0 _j(z)$ for $j \ge 2$, we obtain the expression of $\mathcal{G ^{j_0 _j(z)$ for any index $j \ge 1$: \begin{equation}G^{j_0}_j(z)=\kappa_r(z)^{j-1} \, \kappa_\ell^u(z)^{-j_0-1} \, \dfrac{2 \Big( b_2(z)(m_1(z)+\kappa_\ell^{u}(z)m_2(z))-b_1(z)(m_3(z)+\kappa_\ell^{u}(z)m_4(z)) \Big)}{\alpha_\ell(1-\alpha_\ell) \Delta(z) (\kappa_\ell^{u}(z)-\kappa_\ell(z))} \, .\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-388: Because of the definition of the matrix $\B(z)$, we have the relations: \begin{equation}m_1(z)+\kappa_\ell^{u}(z)m_2(z) =-b_1(z)+(\kappa_\ell^{u}(z)-\kappa_\ell(z)) \, m_2(z) \, , m_3(z)+\kappa_\ell^{u}(z)m_4(z) =-b_2(z)+(\kappa_\ell^{u}(z)-\kappa_\ell(z)) \, m_4(z) \, ,\end{equation} from which we deduce: \begin{equation}\forall \, j \ge 1 \, ,\quad G^{j_0}_j(z)=\kappa_r(z)^{j-1} \, \kappa_\ell^u(z)^{-j_0-1} \, \dfrac{2 \, (b_2(z)m_2(z)-b_1(z)m_4(z))}{\alpha_\ell(1-\alpha_\ell) \Delta(z)} \, ,\end{equation} and we now use the relations: \begin{equation}\label{relationsprop1} b_1(z)=-m_1(z)-\kappa_\ell(z)m_2(z) \, ,\quad b_2(z)=-m_3(z)-\kappa_\ell(z)m_4(z) \, ,\end{equation} to further simplify the expression of $\mathcal{G ^{j_0 _j(z)$, $j \ge 1$, into: \begin{equation}G^{j_0}_j(z)= \, \kappa_r(z)^{j-1} \, \kappa_\ell^u(z)^{-j_0-1} \, \dfrac{2 \, (m_1(z)m_4(z)-m_2(z)m_3(z))}{\alpha_\ell(1-\alpha_\ell) \Delta(z)} = \, \kappa_r(z)^{j-1} \, \kappa_\ell^u(z)^{-j_0-1} \, \dfrac{2 \, \det \M_{2,1}(z) \, \det \M_{1,0}(z)}{\alpha_\ell(1-\alpha_\ell) \Delta(z)} = \, \kappa_r(z)^{j-1} \, \kappa_\ell^u(z)^{-j_0-1} \, \dfrac{2 \, \alpha_\ell (1+\alpha_\ell) \, (1+\alpha_m)}{\alpha_r^2 (1-\alpha_r) (1-\alpha_m) (1-\alpha_\ell) \Delta(z)} \, .\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-389: To obtain the expression \eqref{decompositionGz-2 of $\mathcal{G ^{j_0 _j(z)$ for $j_0 \le -1$ and $j \ge 1$, it only remains to use the relation $\kappa_\ell(z) \, \kappa_\ell^u(z)=-(1+\alpha_\ell)/(1-\alpha_\ell)$ (see \eqref{modekappal ), and the link between the determinant $\Delta(z)$ of $\B(z)$ and $\Delta(z)$ (Lemma \ref{lem3 ).}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-392: We obtain: \bqs \mathcal{G ^{j_0 _j(z)=\left\{ \begin{array {ll \kappa_\ell(z)^{j+1 \, \kappa_\ell^u(z)^{-j_0-1 \, \dfrac{2\left(-\kappa_r(z)(m_1(z)+\kappa_\ell^{u (z)m_2(z))+m_3(z)+\kappa_\ell^{u (z)m_4(z) \right) {\alpha_\ell(1-\alpha_\ell) \Delta(z) (\kappa_\ell^{u (z)-\kappa_\ell(z)) \qquad +\dfrac{2 \, \kappa_\ell^u(z)^{j-j_0 {\alpha_\ell(1-\alpha_\ell)(\kappa_\ell^{u (z)-\kappa_\ell(z)) , j_0< j \leq 0, \kappa_\ell(z)^{j+1 \, \kappa_\ell^u(z)^{-j_0-1 \, \dfrac{2\left(-\kappa_r(z)(m_1(z)+\kappa_\ell^{u (z)m_2(z))+m_3(z)+\kappa_\ell^{u (z)m_4(z) \right) {\alpha_\ell(1-\alpha_\ell) \Delta(z) (\kappa_\ell^{u (z)-\kappa_\ell(z)) \qquad +\dfrac{2 \, \kappa_\ell(z)^{j-j_0 {\alpha_\ell(1-\alpha_\ell)(\kappa_\ell^u(z)-\kappa_\ell(z)) , j\leq j_0.. \end{array \right.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-393: Using the expressions: \begin{equation}\Delta(z)= \, b_2(z)-\kappa_r(z) \, b_1(z)=\kappa_r(z) \, m_1(z)-m_3(z)+\kappa_\ell(z)(\kappa_r(z) \, m_2(z)-m_4(z)) \, , m_1(z)= \, \dfrac{\alpha_\ell (1+\alpha_\ell)}{\alpha_r (1-\alpha_m)} \, ,\quad m_3(z)=-\dfrac{\alpha_\ell (1+\alpha_\ell)}{\alpha_r^2 (1-\alpha_r) (1-\alpha_m)} (2(z-1)+\alpha_r(\alpha_r+\alpha_m)) \, ,\end{equation} we notice that \begin{equation}\dfrac{1}{\kappa_\ell^{u}(z)} \, \dfrac{m_4(z)-\kappa_r(z)m_2(z)}{\alpha_\ell(1-\alpha_\ell)\Delta(z)} =-\dfrac{1}{\alpha_\ell(1+\alpha_\ell)\Delta(z)}\left( -\Delta(z)+\kappa_r(z)m_1(z)-m_3(z)\right) =\dfrac{1}{\alpha_\ell(1+\alpha_\ell)} +\dfrac{1}{\alpha_r(1-\alpha_m)\Delta(z)}\left( -\kappa_r(z) +\frac{2(1-z)-\alpha_r(\alpha_r+\alpha_m)}{\alpha_r(1-\alpha_r)} \right) =-\dfrac{1}{\alpha_\ell(1-\alpha_\ell)\kappa_\ell(z)\kappa_\ell^{u}(z)} +\dfrac{\alpha_r-\alpha_m-(1+\alpha_r) \, \kappa_r(z)^{-1}}{\alpha_r (1-\alpha_r) (1-\alpha_m) \Delta(z)}.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-503: The goal of this chapter is to derive sharp bounds for the temporal Green's function, that is, for any given $j_0 \in \Z$, the solution $(\mathscr{G ^n(j,j_0))_{(n,j) \in \N \times \Z}$ to the recurrence relation: \begin{equation}\label{defgreentemporelle} \begin{cases} \mathscr{G}^{n+1}(j,j_0) \, = \, (\mathscr{L} \, \mathscr{G}^n(\cdot,j_0))_j, (n,j) \in \N \times \Z \, , \mathscr{G}^0(\cdot,j_0) \, = \, \boldsymbol{\delta}_{j_0} \, . \end{cases}\end{equation} The relevance of this sequence is motivated by the fact that for any initial condition $\mathbf{h \in \ell^q(\Z;\R)$, the solution to the recurrence relation: \begin{equation}\forall \, n \in \N \, ,\quad v^{n+1} \, = \, \mathscr{L} \, v^n \, , v^0 \, = \, h \, ,\end{equation} can be decomposed into: \begin{equation}\forall \, (n,j) \in \N \times \Z \, ,\quad v^n_j \, = \, \sum_{j_0 \in \Z} \, \mathscr{G}^n(j,j_0) \, h_{j_0} \, ,\end{equation} and we expect that sharp bounds on $\mathscr{G ^n(j,j_0)$ will quantify the decay properties of the semigroup of operators $(\mathscr{L ^n)_{n \in \N}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-504: We first decompose the temporal Green's function by following the decomposition given in Proposition \ref{prop3 for the (reduced) spatial Green's function.}
elements.sent-2411-505: We then analyze each contribution in the decomposition and derive bounds that are meant to be as sharp as possible in order to obtain large time decaying bounds for the semigroup $(\mathscr{L ^n)_{n \in \N}$.}
elements.sent-2411-506: We always consider from now on that the weak solution \eqref{shock satisfies the entropy inequalities \eqref{entropy and that the CFL parameter $\lambda$ satisfies the stability condition \eqref{CFL .}}}
elements.sent-2411-507: We also assume that Assumption \ref{hyp-stabspectrale is satisfied so that the analysis of Chapter \ref{chapter3 can be used.}}
elements.sent-2411-508: The value of the temporal Green's function $\mathscr{G ^n(j,j_0)$ is given by the so-called functional calculus (see \cite{Conway ): \begin{equation}\label{formuleGjn} \mathscr{G}^n(j,j_0) \, = \, \dfrac{1}{2 \pi \mbi} \, \int_{\widetilde{\Gamma}} z^n \, \mathcal{G}^{j_0}_j(z) \, \md z,\end{equation} where $\widetilde{\Gamma}$ is any contour that encompasses the spectrum of the operator $\mathscr{L}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-509: In view of Theorem \ref{thm1bis , one can choose for instance $\widetilde{\Gamma =(1+\delta) \, \cercle=\{ \zeta \in \C \, | \, |\zeta|=1+\delta \}$ for any $\delta>0$, since the spectrum of $\mathscr{L}$ is included in $\D \cup \{ 1 \}$.}}

elements.sent-2411-511: The associated temporal Green's functions for these operators are defined as the solutions to the recurrences: \begin{subequations \begin{equation} \overline{\mathscr{G}}_\ell^{\, n+1} \, = \, \mathscr{L}_\ell \, \overline{\mathscr{G}}_\ell^{\, n}, n \in \N \, , \overline{\mathscr{G}}_\ell^{\, 0} \, = \, \delta_0 \, ,  \label{defgreentemporellel}  \overline{\mathscr{G}}_r^{\, n+1} \, = \, \mathscr{L}_r \, \overline{\mathscr{G}}_r^{\, n}, n \in \N \, , \overline{\mathscr{G}}_r^{\, 0} \, = \, \delta_0 \, ,  \label{defgreentemporeller}\end{equation} \end{subequations and the same functional calculus rules as before yield the expressions: \begin{equation}\overline{\mathscr{G}}_\ell^{\, n}(j) \, = \, \dfrac{1}{2 \pi \mbi} \, \int_{\widetilde{\Gamma}} z^n \, \overline{G}_{\ell,j}(z) \, \md z \, ,\quad \overline{\mathscr{G}}_r^{\, n}(j) \, = \, \dfrac{1}{2 \pi \mbi} \, \int_{\widetilde{\Gamma}} z^n \, \overline{G}_{r,j}(z) \, \md z \, ,\end{equation} where $\widetilde{\Gamma}$ is once again any closed contour that encompasses the spectrum of both $\mathscr{L _\ell$ and $\mathscr{L _r$ in its interior.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-533: We first introduce some constants: \begin{subequations \begin{equation}c_{3,\ell} \, := \, \dfrac{\alpha_\ell \, (1 - \alpha_\ell^2)}{6} >0 \, ,\quad c_{4,\ell} \, := \, \dfrac{\alpha_\ell^2 \, (1 - \alpha_\ell^2)}{8} \, > \, 0 \, ,\label{defc3c4l} c_{3,r} \, := \, \dfrac{\alpha_r \, (1 - \alpha_r^2)}{6} < 0 \, ,\quad c_{4,r} \, := \, \dfrac{\alpha_r^2 \, (1 - \alpha_r^2)}{8} \, > \, 0 \, .\label{defc3c4r}\end{equation} \end{subequations We then define the following two functions\footnote{The letter $A$ refers to ``activation'' in analogy with \cite{Coeuret1 .}}}}

elements.sent-2411-568: Upon denoting \begin{equation}\label{defomegazeta} \omega \, := \, \dfrac{j_0-n \, |\alpha_r|}{n} \in \, \left[ -\frac{|\alpha_r|}{2},1-|\alpha_r|\right] \quad \text{ and } \quad \zeta:= \dfrac{j_0}{n} \, \dfrac{1-\alpha_r^2}{2 \, \alpha_r^2} \in \left[ \frac{1-\alpha_r^2}{4 \, |\alpha_r|},\frac{1-\alpha_r^2}{2 \, \alpha_r^2}\right] \, ,\end{equation} we get \begin{equation}\label{relationomegazeta} n \, \tau-j_0 \, \varphi_r(\tau) \, = \, \dfrac{n}{|\alpha_r|} \, \left[ - \omega \, \tau + \dfrac{\zeta}{3} \, \tau^3 - \dfrac{\zeta}{4} \, \tau^4 \right] \, .\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-580: Cauchy's formula shows that the value of the integral: \begin{equation}\dfrac{1}{2\pi i} \int_{\eta +\mbi \R} \rme^{n \, \tau-j_0 \, \varphi_r(\tau)} \frac{\md \tau}{\tau}\end{equation} does not depend on $\eta>0$ so choosing the parametrization $\tau=|\alpha_r| \, (\eta+\mbi \, \theta)$ in the integral, we get: \begin{equation}\frac{1}{2\pi i} \int_{\eta+\mbi\R} \rme^{n \, \tau-j_0 \, \varphi_r(\tau) } \frac{\md \tau}{\tau} \ = \, \dfrac{1}{2 \, \pi} \, \int_\R {\rm e}^{-(j_0+n\, \alpha_r) \, (\eta+\mbi \, \theta)} \, {\rm e}^{\, j_0 \, \frac{1-\alpha_r^2}{6} \, (\eta+\mbi \, \theta)^3} \, {\rm e}^{j_0 \, \alpha_r \, \frac{1-\alpha_r^2}{8} \, (\eta+\mbi \, \theta)^4} \, \dfrac{{\rm d}\theta}{\eta+\mbi \, \theta} \, .\end{equation}.

elements.subsection-2411-006: Final decomposition of the temporal Green's function. Proof of Theorem \ref{thmGreen}
elements.sent-2411-694: Let us recall that, for $j_0 \ge 1$, we have first decomposed (see \eqref{decomposition-initial ): \begin{equation}\mathscr{G}^n(j,j_0) \, = \, \overline{\mathscr{G}}_r^n(j-j_0) \, \mathds{1}_{j \ge 1} \, + \, \widetilde{\mathscr{G}}^n(j,j_0) \, ,\end{equation} and the reduced Green's function $\widetilde{\mathscr{G ^n(j,j_0)$ has been decomposed into: \begin{equation}\widetilde{\mathscr{G}}^n(j,j_0) \, = \, \dfrac{1}{2 \pi \mbi} \, \int_{\Gamma_{\rm out}} \rme^{n \, \tau} \, \widetilde{G}^{j_0}_j(\rme^\tau) \, \rme^\tau \, \md \tau \, + \, \dfrac{1}{2 \pi \mbi} \, \int_{\Gamma_{\rm in}} \rme^{n \, \tau} \, \widetilde{G}^{j_0}_j(\rme^\tau) \, \rme^\tau \, \md \tau \, ,\end{equation} with suitable contours $\Gamma_{\rm out}$ and $\Gamma_{\rm in}$.}}}

elements.section-2411-012: Derivative of the temporal Green's functions
elements.sent-2411-706: As it will be made clear in the last chapter of this article, it will also be necessary to obtain large time decaying bounds for the family of operators $\left(\cL^n(\mathrm{Id -\mathbf{S )\right)_{n\in\N}$ where $\mathbf{S :\ell^q(\Z;\R)\to\ell^q(\Z;\R)$ is the shift operator defined as $(\mathbf{S}\bfh )_j:=h_{j+1}$ for all $j\in\Z$ for any sequence $\bfh =(h_j)_{j \in \Z \in\ell^q(\Z;\R)$.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-743: For any $\gamma_2 \ge \gamma_1 \ge 0$, there exists $C_\cL(\gamma_1,\gamma_2)>0$ such that we have the following estimates on the semigroup $(\cL^n)_{n\in\N}$: \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\forall n \in\N \,, \quad \left\|\cL^n \, \bfh \right\|_{\ell^1_{\gamma_1}} \, \leq \, \frac{C_\cL(\gamma_1,\gamma_2)}{(1+n)^{\gamma_2-\gamma_1-1/8}}\, \|\bfh\|_{\ell^1_{\gamma_2}}\,,\quad  for  \bfh\in\ell^1_{\gamma_2}(\Z;\R) with  \sum_{j\in\Z}h_j=0\, ,\label{estimLn1} \forall n \in\N \,, \quad \left\|\cL^n \, \bfh \right\|_{\ell^\infty_{\gamma_1}} \, \leq \, \frac{C_\cL(\gamma_1,\gamma_2)}{(1+n)^{\gamma_2-\gamma_1+\min\left(1/3,\gamma_1\right)}} \, \| \bfh\|_{\ell^1_{\gamma_2}}\,,\quad  for  \bfh\in\ell^1_{\gamma_2}(\Z;\R) with  \sum_{j\in\Z}h_j=0\, ,\label{estimLn2}\end{equation} \end{subequations and the following estimates on the family of operators $(\cL^n(\mathrm{Id -\mathbf{S ))_{n\in\N}$: \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\forall n \in\N \,, \quad\left\|\cL^n(Id-S) \bfh \right\|_{\ell^1_{\gamma_1}} \leq \frac{C_\cL(\gamma_1,\gamma_2)}{(1+n)^{\gamma_2-\gamma_1+1/8}}\,\|\bfh\|_{\ell^1_{\gamma_2}}\, ,\quad  for  \bfh\in\ell^1_{\gamma_2}(\Z;\R)\,, \label{estimLnshift1} \forall n \in\N \,, \quad\left\|\cL^n(Id-S) \bfh \right\|_{\ell^\infty_{\gamma_1}} \leq \frac{C_\cL(\gamma_1,\gamma_2)}{(1+n)^{\gamma_2-\gamma_1+1/3+\min\left(1/4,\gamma_1\right)}}\, \|\bfh\|_{\ell^1_{\gamma_2}}\, ,\quad  for  \bfh\in\ell^1_{\gamma_2}(\Z;\R)\,,\label{estimLnshift2} \forall n \in\N \,, \quad\left\|\cL^n(Id-S) \bfh \right\|_{\ell^\infty_{\gamma_1}} \leq \frac{C_\cL(\gamma_1,\gamma_2)}{(1+n)^{\gamma_2-\gamma_1+\min\left(1/8,\gamma_1-1/8\right)}}\,\|\bfh\|_{\ell^\infty_{\gamma_2}}\, ,\quad  for  \bfh\in\ell^\infty_{\gamma_2}(\Z;\R)\, .\label{estimLnshift3}\end{equation} \end{subequations.}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-202: \begin{equation}\forall n \in\N \,, \quad \left\|\cL^n \, \bfh \right\|_{\ell^1_{\gamma_1}} \, \leq \, \frac{C_\cL(\gamma_1,\gamma_2)}{(1+n)^{\gamma_2-\gamma_1-1/8}}\, \|\bfh\|_{\ell^1_{\gamma_2}}\,,\quad  for  \bfh\in\ell^1_{\gamma_2}(\Z;\R) with  \sum_{j\in\Z}h_j=0\, ,\label{estimLn1} \forall n \in\N \,, \quad \left\|\cL^n \, \bfh \right\|_{\ell^\infty_{\gamma_1}} \, \leq \, \frac{C_\cL(\gamma_1,\gamma_2)}{(1+n)^{\gamma_2-\gamma_1+\min\left(1/3,\gamma_1\right)}} \, \| \bfh\|_{\ell^1_{\gamma_2}}\,,\quad  for  \bfh\in\ell^1_{\gamma_2}(\Z;\R) with  \sum_{j\in\Z}h_j=0\, ,\label{estimLn2}\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-203: \begin{equation}\forall n \in\N \,, \quad\left\|\cL^n(Id-S) \bfh \right\|_{\ell^1_{\gamma_1}} \leq \frac{C_\cL(\gamma_1,\gamma_2)}{(1+n)^{\gamma_2-\gamma_1+1/8}}\,\|\bfh\|_{\ell^1_{\gamma_2}}\, ,\quad  for  \bfh\in\ell^1_{\gamma_2}(\Z;\R)\,, \label{estimLnshift1} \forall n \in\N \,, \quad\left\|\cL^n(Id-S) \bfh \right\|_{\ell^\infty_{\gamma_1}} \leq \frac{C_\cL(\gamma_1,\gamma_2)}{(1+n)^{\gamma_2-\gamma_1+1/3+\min\left(1/4,\gamma_1\right)}}\, \|\bfh\|_{\ell^1_{\gamma_2}}\, ,\quad  for  \bfh\in\ell^1_{\gamma_2}(\Z;\R)\,,\label{estimLnshift2} \forall n \in\N \,, \quad\left\|\cL^n(Id-S) \bfh \right\|_{\ell^\infty_{\gamma_1}} \leq \frac{C_\cL(\gamma_1,\gamma_2)}{(1+n)^{\gamma_2-\gamma_1+\min\left(1/8,\gamma_1-1/8\right)}}\,\|\bfh\|_{\ell^\infty_{\gamma_2}}\, ,\quad  for  \bfh\in\ell^\infty_{\gamma_2}(\Z;\R)\, .\label{estimLnshift3}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-744: For $\gamma\geq0$ and $\bfh\in \ell^q_\gamma(\Z;\R)$ we recall that the action of the semigroup $\cL^n$ on $\bfh$ is given for each $n\in\N$ by \bqs \forall j \in\Z \, , \quad \left(\cL^n \mathbf{h}\right)_j= \sum_{j_0\in\Z}\cG^n(j,j_0)h_{j_0}\,, \eqs where $\cG^n(\cdot,j_0)$ is the temporal Green's function solution of \eqref{defgreentemporelle .}
elements.sent-2411-745: The bounds \eqref{estimLn1 -\eqref{estimLn2 are trivial for $n=0$ since we have $\gamma_1 \le \gamma_2$ and therefore $\|\bfh\|_{\ell^1_{\gamma_1 \le \|\bfh\|_{\ell^1_{\gamma_2 }$ so we assume from now on $n \in \N^*$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-746: Motivated by the estimates obtained on the temporal Green's function in Theorem~\ref{thmGreen (for $n \in \N^*$), we introduce a family of operators $(\cL^n_{\mathrm{act )_{n\in\N^*}$ acting on a given sequence $\bfh\in \ell^q_\gamma (\Z;\R)$ as follows: \begin{equation}\forall (n,j) \in \N^*\times \Z\,,\quad \left(\cL^n_{act} \, \bfh \right)_j := \, \, H_j \, \sum_{j_0\geq1}\mathds{1}_{|j-j_0|\leq n} \, A_r(-j_0+n \, |\alpha_r|,n) \, h_{j_0} +H_j \, \sum_{j_0\leq0} \mathds{1}_{|j-j_0|\leq n} \, A_\ell(j_0+n \, \alpha_\ell,n) \, h_{j_0} \, .\end{equation}.}}}
elements.formula-2411-204: \begin{equation}\forall (n,j) \in \N^*\times \Z\,,\quad \left(\cL^n_{act} \, \bfh \right)_j := \, \, H_j \, \sum_{j_0\geq1}\mathds{1}_{|j-j_0|\leq n} \, A_r(-j_0+n \, |\alpha_r|,n) \, h_{j_0} +H_j \, \sum_{j_0\leq0} \mathds{1}_{|j-j_0|\leq n} \, A_\ell(j_0+n \, \alpha_\ell,n) \, h_{j_0} \, .\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-747: It is important to observe that $\cL^n_{\mathrm{act }$ does not stand for the $n$-th power of $\cL^1_{\mathrm{act }$, while $\cL^n$ is the $n$-th power of $\cL$.}}
elements.sent-2411-748: However, we hope that this will not create any confusion for the reader and we keep the notation as such to bear in mind that all operators are considered at the discrete time $n$.
elements.sent-2411-749: We recall that the Green's function satisfies $\cG^n(j,j_0)=0$ for $|j-j_0|>n$.
elements.sent-2411-750: With the previous definition for $\cL^n_{\mathrm{act }$, for all $(n,j) \in \N^*\times \Z$, one can therefore decompose \begin{equation}\left(\cL^n h\right)_j=\left(\cL^n_{act} \bfh\right)_j +\sum_{j_0\geq1} \left(\cG^n(j,j_0)-\mathds{1}_{|j-j_0|\leq n} \, H_j \, A_r(-j_0+n \, |\alpha_r|,n)\right) \, h_{j_0} \notag + \sum_{j_0\leq0} \left(\cG^n(j,j_0)-\mathds{1}_{|j-j_0|\leq n} \, H_j \, A_\ell(j_0+n \, \alpha_\ell,n)\right) \, h_{j_0} \, . \label{decompositionlin-n}\end{equation} We now introduce some notation.}
elements.formula-2411-205: \begin{equation}\left(\cL^n h\right)_j=\left(\cL^n_{act} \bfh\right)_j +\sum_{j_0\geq1} \left(\cG^n(j,j_0)-\mathds{1}_{|j-j_0|\leq n} \, H_j \, A_r(-j_0+n \, |\alpha_r|,n)\right) \, h_{j_0} \notag + \sum_{j_0\leq0} \left(\cG^n(j,j_0)-\mathds{1}_{|j-j_0|\leq n} \, H_j \, A_\ell(j_0+n \, \alpha_\ell,n)\right) \, h_{j_0} \, . \label{decompositionlin-n}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-751: For $(n,j) \in \N^* \times \Z$ and $\bfh \in \ell^1_{\gamma_2 (\Z;\R)$, we define: \begin{equation}S_{r,1}(n,j,\bfh) \, \, := \, \mathds{1}_{j\ge1} \, \sum_{j_0\ge1} \, \mathds{1}_{|j-j_0|\leq n} \, M_r \left( c,j-j_0+n \, |\alpha_r|,n \right) \, \left|h_{j_0}\right| \, , S_{\ell,1}(n,j,\bfh) \, \, := \, \mathds{1}_{j\le0} \, \sum_{j_0\le0} \, \mathds{1}_{|j-j_0|\leq n} \, M_\ell \left( c,j_0-j+n \, \alpha_\ell,n\right) \, \left|h_{j_0}\right| \, ,\end{equation} and \begin{equation}S_{r,2}(n,j,\bfh) \, \, := \, \rme^{-c \, |j|} \, \sum_{j_0\ge1} \, M_r \left( c,-j_0+n \, |\alpha_r|,n \right) \, \left|h_{j_0}\right| \, , S_{\ell,2}(n,j,\bfh) \, \, := \, \rme^{-c \, |j|} \, \sum_{j_0\le0} \, M_\ell \left( c,j_0+n \, \alpha_\ell,n \right) \, \left|h_{j_0}\right| \, ,\end{equation} where we recall that the functions $\mathbf{M _\ell$ and $\mathbf{M _r$ are defined in \eqref{defmajorant .}}}}
elements.formula-2411-206: \begin{equation}S_{r,1}(n,j,\bfh) \, \, := \, \mathds{1}_{j\ge1} \, \sum_{j_0\ge1} \, \mathds{1}_{|j-j_0|\leq n} \, M_r \left( c,j-j_0+n \, |\alpha_r|,n \right) \, \left|h_{j_0}\right| \, , S_{\ell,1}(n,j,\bfh) \, \, := \, \mathds{1}_{j\le0} \, \sum_{j_0\le0} \, \mathds{1}_{|j-j_0|\leq n} \, M_\ell \left( c,j_0-j+n \, \alpha_\ell,n\right) \, \left|h_{j_0}\right| \, ,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-753: Combining those two inequalities with the triangle inequality in \eqref{decompositionlin-n , we have for all $(n,j) \in \N^*\times \Z$: \begin{equation}\label{estimationlin-n} \left| \left( \cL^n \bfh \right)_j \right| \le \left| \left( \cL^n_{\mathrm{act}} \bfh \right)_j \right| \, + \, C \, \Big( \mathfrak{S}_{r,1}(n,j,\bfh) + \mathfrak{S}_{\ell,1}(n,j,\bfh) + \mathfrak{S}_{r,2}(n,j,\bfh) + \mathfrak{S}_{\ell,2}(n,j,\bfh) +\mathfrak{S}_{\mathrm{exp}}(n,j,\bfh) \Big) \, .\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-754: In order to prove the estimates \eqref{estimLn1 and \eqref{estimLn2 , one simply needs to prove estimates for each of the terms appearing in the right-hand side of the above inequality.}}
elements.sent-2411-755: From now on, we shall proceed term by term.
elements.sent-2411-757: For any $\gamma_2 \ge \gamma_1 \ge 0$, there exists $C>0$, such that for all $n\geq1$ and $\bfh \in \ell^1_{\gamma_2 (\Z;\R)$, one has: \begin{equation}\left\| \left( S_{r,1}(n,j,\bfh) \right)_{j\in\Z} \right\|_{\ell^1_{\gamma_1}} + \left\| \left( S_{\ell,1}(n,j,\bfh) \right)_{j\in\Z} \right\|_{\ell^1_{\gamma_1}} \, \leq \, \dfrac{C}{n^{\gamma_2-\gamma_1-1/8}} \, \| \bfh \|_{\ell^1_{\gamma_2}} \, , \left\| \left( S_{r,1}(n,j,\bfh) \right)_{j\in\Z} \right\|_{\ell^\infty_{\gamma_1}} + \left\| \left( S_{\ell,1}(n,j,\bfh)\right)_{j\in\Z} \right\|_{\ell^\infty_{\gamma_1}} \, \leq \, \dfrac{C}{n^{\gamma_2-\gamma_1+1/3}} \, \| \bfh \|_{\ell^1_{\gamma_2}} \, .\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-758: We only prove the estimates for $\left(\mathfrak{S _{r,1 (n,j,\bfh)\right)_{j\in\Z}$, the other case is handled similarly.}}
elements.sent-2411-759: For $\bfh \in \ell^1_{\gamma_2 (\Z;\R)$ and $n \in \N^*$, we have that \begin{equation}\left\| \left( S_{r,1}(n,j,\bfh) \right)_{j\in\Z} \right\|_{\ell^1_{\gamma_1}} = \, \, \sum_{j\in\Z} \, (1+|j|^{\gamma_1}) \, \mathds{1}_{j\geq1} \, \left( \sum_{j_0\geq1} \, \mathds{1}_{|j-j_0|\leq n} \, M_r \left( c,j-j_0+n \, |\alpha_r|,n \right) \, \left| h_{j_0} \right| \right) = \, \, \underbrace{\sum_{j\ge 1} \, (1+|j|^{\gamma_1}) \, \left( \sum_{j_0=1}^j \, \mathds{1}_{|j-j_0|\leq n} \, M_r \left( c,j-j_0+n \, |\alpha_r|,n \right) \left| h_{j_0} \right| \right)}_{=: \mathscr{I}^n_1} \, +\underbrace{\sum_{j\ge1} \, (1+|j|^{\gamma_1}) \, \left( \sum_{j_0 \geq j+1} \, \mathds{1}_{|j-j_0|\leq n} \, M_r \left( c,j-j_0+n \, |\alpha_r|,n \right) \, \left| h_{j_0} \right| \right)}_{=:\mathscr{I}^n_2} \, .\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-210: \begin{equation}\left\| \left( S_{r,1}(n,j,\bfh) \right)_{j\in\Z} \right\|_{\ell^1_{\gamma_1}} = \, \, \sum_{j\in\Z} \, (1+|j|^{\gamma_1}) \, \mathds{1}_{j\geq1} \, \left( \sum_{j_0\geq1} \, \mathds{1}_{|j-j_0|\leq n} \, M_r \left( c,j-j_0+n \, |\alpha_r|,n \right) \, \left| h_{j_0} \right| \right) = \, \, \underbrace{\sum_{j\ge 1} \, (1+|j|^{\gamma_1}) \, \left( \sum_{j_0=1}^j \, \mathds{1}_{|j-j_0|\leq n} \, M_r \left( c,j-j_0+n \, |\alpha_r|,n \right) \left| h_{j_0} \right| \right)}_{=: \mathscr{I}^n_1} \, +\underbrace{\sum_{j\ge1} \, (1+|j|^{\gamma_1}) \, \left( \sum_{j_0 \geq j+1} \, \mathds{1}_{|j-j_0|\leq n} \, M_r \left( c,j-j_0+n \, |\alpha_r|,n \right) \, \left| h_{j_0} \right| \right)}_{=:\mathscr{I}^n_2} \, .\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-760: From the definition \eqref{defmajorantr of $\mathbf{M _r$, we note that for $1\leq j_0 \leq j$ with $|j-j_0|\leq n$, one has \bqs \mathbf{M _r\left(c,j-j_0+n \, |\alpha_r|,n\right)\leq C \rme^{-c \, n \, .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-761: We thus obtain the following estimate for the first contribution $\mathscr{I ^n_1$: \begin{equation}\mathscr{I}^n_1 \le \, C \, (1+n^{\gamma_1}) \, \rme^{-c \, n} \, \sum_{j\ge1} \, \sum_{j_0=1}^j \, \mathds{1}_{|j-j_0|\leq n} \, (1+|j_0|)^{\gamma_1} \, |h_{j_0}| = \, C \, (1+n^{\gamma_1}) \, \rme^{-c \, n} \, \sum_{j_0\ge1} \, \left( \sum_{j\geq j_0} \, \mathds{1}_{|j-j_0|\leq n} \right) \, (1+|j_0|^{\gamma_1}) \, |h_{j_0}| \le \, C \, n \, (1+n^{\gamma_1}) \, \rme^{-c \, n} \, \|\bfh\|_{\ell^1_{\gamma_1}} \, \le \, C \, \rme^{-c \, n} \, \|\bfh\|_{\ell^1_{\gamma_2}} \, .\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-211: \begin{equation}\mathscr{I}^n_1 \le \, C \, (1+n^{\gamma_1}) \, \rme^{-c \, n} \, \sum_{j\ge1} \, \sum_{j_0=1}^j \, \mathds{1}_{|j-j_0|\leq n} \, (1+|j_0|)^{\gamma_1} \, |h_{j_0}| = \, C \, (1+n^{\gamma_1}) \, \rme^{-c \, n} \, \sum_{j_0\ge1} \, \left( \sum_{j\geq j_0} \, \mathds{1}_{|j-j_0|\leq n} \right) \, (1+|j_0|^{\gamma_1}) \, |h_{j_0}| \le \, C \, n \, (1+n^{\gamma_1}) \, \rme^{-c \, n} \, \|\bfh\|_{\ell^1_{\gamma_1}} \, \le \, C \, \rme^{-c \, n} \, \|\bfh\|_{\ell^1_{\gamma_2}} \, .\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-762: On the other hand, for the second contribution $\mathscr{I ^n_2$, we decompose the sum into two parts: \begin{equation}\mathscr{I}^n_2 = \, \, \sum_{j\ge1} \, (1+|j|^{\gamma_1}) \, \left( \sum_{j_0\geq j+1} \, \mathds{1}_{j_0< \frac{n \, |\alpha_r|}{2}} \, \mathds{1}_{|j-j_0|\leq n} \, M_r \left( c,j-j_0+n \, |\alpha_r|,n \right) \, \left| h_{j_0} \right| \right) \, + \sum_{j\ge1} \, (1+|j|^{\gamma_1}) \, \left( \sum_{j_0\geq j+1} \, \mathds{1}_{j_0 \geq \frac{n \, |\alpha_r|}{2}} \, \mathds{1}_{|j-j_0|\leq n} \, M_r \left( c,j-j_0+n \, |\alpha_r|,n \right) \left| h_{j_0} \right| \right) \, .\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-212: \begin{equation}\mathscr{I}^n_2 = \, \, \sum_{j\ge1} \, (1+|j|^{\gamma_1}) \, \left( \sum_{j_0\geq j+1} \, \mathds{1}_{j_0< \frac{n \, |\alpha_r|}{2}} \, \mathds{1}_{|j-j_0|\leq n} \, M_r \left( c,j-j_0+n \, |\alpha_r|,n \right) \, \left| h_{j_0} \right| \right) \, + \sum_{j\ge1} \, (1+|j|^{\gamma_1}) \, \left( \sum_{j_0\geq j+1} \, \mathds{1}_{j_0 \geq \frac{n \, |\alpha_r|}{2}} \, \mathds{1}_{|j-j_0|\leq n} \, M_r \left( c,j-j_0+n \, |\alpha_r|,n \right) \left| h_{j_0} \right| \right) \, .\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-763: In both sums, since we have $1 \le j \le j_0$, we can always use the inequality: \begin{equation}(1+|j|^{\gamma_1}) \, \le \, (1+|j_0|^{\gamma_1}) \, .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-764: Furthermore, we notice once again that for $j\ge1$ and $j_0\geq j+1$ with $1\leq j_0 < \frac{n \, |\alpha_r| {2}$, one has an exponential bound: \bqs \mathbf{M _r\left(c,j-j_0+n \, |\alpha_r|,n\right) \, \leq \, C \, \rme^{-c \, n \, .}}}
elements.sent-2411-765: For the remaining contribution, we have \begin{equation}\sum_{j\ge1} (1+|j|^{\gamma_1}) \, \left( \sum_{j_0\geq j+1} \, \mathds{1}_{ j_0 \geq \frac{n \, |\alpha_r|}{2}} \, \mathds{1}_{|j-j_0|\leq n} \, M_r \left( c,j-j_0+n \, |\alpha_r|,n \right) \, \left| h_{j_0} \right| \right) \le \, \sum_{j_0\geq1} \, \mathds{1}_{ j_0\geq \frac{n \, |\alpha_r|}{2}} \, \left( \sum_{j=1}^{j_0} M_r \left( c,j-j_0+n \, |\alpha_r|,n \right) \right) \, (1+|j_0|^{\gamma_1}) \, |h_{j_0}| \le \, C \, \|M_r\left(c,\cdot+n \, |\alpha_r|,n\right)\|_{\ell^1(\Z)} \, \sum_{j_0 \geq 1} \, \mathds{1}_{ j_0 \geq \frac{n \, |\alpha_r|}{2}} \, \dfrac{(1+|j_0|^{\gamma_2})}{(1+|j_0|^{\gamma_2-\gamma_1})}|h_{j_0}| \le \, \frac{C}{n^{\gamma_2-\gamma_1}} \, \|M_r\left(c,\cdot+n \, |\alpha_r|,n\right)\|_{\ell^1(\Z)} \, \| \bfh \|_{\ell^1_{\gamma_2}} \, .\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-214: \begin{equation}\sum_{j\ge1} (1+|j|^{\gamma_1}) \, \left( \sum_{j_0\geq j+1} \, \mathds{1}_{ j_0 \geq \frac{n \, |\alpha_r|}{2}} \, \mathds{1}_{|j-j_0|\leq n} \, M_r \left( c,j-j_0+n \, |\alpha_r|,n \right) \, \left| h_{j_0} \right| \right) \le \, \sum_{j_0\geq1} \, \mathds{1}_{ j_0\geq \frac{n \, |\alpha_r|}{2}} \, \left( \sum_{j=1}^{j_0} M_r \left( c,j-j_0+n \, |\alpha_r|,n \right) \right) \, (1+|j_0|^{\gamma_1}) \, |h_{j_0}| \le \, C \, \|M_r\left(c,\cdot+n \, |\alpha_r|,n\right)\|_{\ell^1(\Z)} \, \sum_{j_0 \geq 1} \, \mathds{1}_{ j_0 \geq \frac{n \, |\alpha_r|}{2}} \, \dfrac{(1+|j_0|^{\gamma_2})}{(1+|j_0|^{\gamma_2-\gamma_1})}|h_{j_0}| \le \, \frac{C}{n^{\gamma_2-\gamma_1}} \, \|M_r\left(c,\cdot+n \, |\alpha_r|,n\right)\|_{\ell^1(\Z)} \, \| \bfh \|_{\ell^1_{\gamma_2}} \, .\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-766: In order to conclude, we just use the following estimate that can be obtained from the definition \eqref{defmajorantr and a mere comparison between a series and an integral: \begin{equation}\label{estiml1Mr} \forall \, n \in \N^* \, ,\quad \left\| \left( \mathbf{M}_r \left( c,j+n \, |\alpha_r|,n \right) \right)_{j\in\Z} \right\|_{\ell^1(\Z)} \, \le \, C \, n^{1/8} \, ,\end{equation} where, of course, all constants are uniform with respect to $n \in \N^*$.}
elements.sent-2411-767: The proof of the $\ell^1_{\gamma_1}$ estimate for $\mathfrak{S _{r,1 (n,\cdot,\bfh)$ is now complete.}}
elements.sent-2411-768: We now turn our attention to the second estimate in $\ell^\infty_{\gamma_1}$.
elements.sent-2411-769: By definition of the norm in $\ell^\infty_{\gamma_1}$, we have \begin{equation}\left\| \left( S_{r,1}(n,j,\bfh) \right)_{j\in\Z} \right\|_{\ell^\infty_{\gamma_1}} = \, \, \sup_{j\in\Z} \, (1+|j|^{\gamma_1}) \, \mathds{1}_{j\geq1} \, \left( \sum_{j_0\geq1} \, \mathds{1}_{|j-j_0|\leq n} \, M_r \left( c,j-j_0+n \, |\alpha_r|,n \right) \, \left| h_{j_0} \right| \right) \le \, \, \sup_{j\ge1} \, (1+|j|^{\gamma_1}) \, \left( \sum_{j_0=1}^j \, \mathds{1}_{|j-j_0|\leq n} \, M_r \left( c,j-j_0+n \, |\alpha_r|,n \right) \, \left| h_{j_0} \right| \right) + \sup_{j\ge1} \, (1+|j|^{\gamma_1}) \, \left( \sum_{j_0\geq j+1} \, \mathds{1}_{j_0< \frac{n \, |\alpha_r|}{2}} \, \mathds{1}_{|j-j_0|\leq n} \, M_r \left( c,j-j_0+n \, |\alpha_r|,n \right) \, \left| h_{j_0} \right| \right) + \sup_{j\ge1} \, (1+|j|^{\gamma_1}) \, \left( \sum_{j_0\geq j+1} \, \mathds{1}_{j_0\geq \frac{n \, |\alpha_r|}{2}} \, \mathds{1}_{|j-j_0|\leq n} \, M_r \left( c,j-j_0+n \, |\alpha_r|,n \right) \, \left| h_{j_0} \right| \right) =:\mathscr{J}^n_1+\mathscr{J}^n_2+\mathscr{J}^n_3 \, .\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-216: \begin{equation}\left\| \left( S_{r,1}(n,j,\bfh) \right)_{j\in\Z} \right\|_{\ell^\infty_{\gamma_1}} = \, \, \sup_{j\in\Z} \, (1+|j|^{\gamma_1}) \, \mathds{1}_{j\geq1} \, \left( \sum_{j_0\geq1} \, \mathds{1}_{|j-j_0|\leq n} \, M_r \left( c,j-j_0+n \, |\alpha_r|,n \right) \, \left| h_{j_0} \right| \right) \le \, \, \sup_{j\ge1} \, (1+|j|^{\gamma_1}) \, \left( \sum_{j_0=1}^j \, \mathds{1}_{|j-j_0|\leq n} \, M_r \left( c,j-j_0+n \, |\alpha_r|,n \right) \, \left| h_{j_0} \right| \right) + \sup_{j\ge1} \, (1+|j|^{\gamma_1}) \, \left( \sum_{j_0\geq j+1} \, \mathds{1}_{j_0< \frac{n \, |\alpha_r|}{2}} \, \mathds{1}_{|j-j_0|\leq n} \, M_r \left( c,j-j_0+n \, |\alpha_r|,n \right) \, \left| h_{j_0} \right| \right) + \sup_{j\ge1} \, (1+|j|^{\gamma_1}) \, \left( \sum_{j_0\geq j+1} \, \mathds{1}_{j_0\geq \frac{n \, |\alpha_r|}{2}} \, \mathds{1}_{|j-j_0|\leq n} \, M_r \left( c,j-j_0+n \, |\alpha_r|,n \right) \, \left| h_{j_0} \right| \right) =:\mathscr{J}^n_1+\mathscr{J}^n_2+\mathscr{J}^n_3 \, .\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-770: Performing similar computations and estimates as above, one gets first: \bqs \mathscr{J ^n_1 \, + \, \mathscr{J ^n_2 \, \le \, C \, \rme^{-c \, n \, \|\bfh\|_{\ell^1_{\gamma_2 \, , \eqs which is an even better estimate than the one we are aiming at.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-771: On the other hand, we have \begin{equation}\mathscr{J}^n_3 \, \le \, C \, \|M_r\left(c,\cdot+n \, |\alpha_r|,n\right)\|_{\ell^\infty(\Z)} \, \sum_{j_0 \geq \frac{n \, |\alpha_r|}{2}} \, (1+|j_0|^{\gamma_1}) \, \left| h_{j_0} \right| \le \, \frac{C}{n^{\gamma_2-\gamma_1}} \, \|M_r\left(c,\cdot+n \, |\alpha_r|,n\right)\|_{\ell^\infty(\Z)} \, \|\bfh\|_{\ell^1_{\gamma_2}} \, ,\end{equation} and it simply remains to use the property: \begin{equation}\label{estimlinftyMr} \left\| \left( \mathbf{M}_r \left( c,j+n \, |\alpha_r|,n \right) \right)_{j\in\Z} \right\|_{\ell^\infty(\Z)} \, \le \, \dfrac{1}{n^{1/3}} \, ,\end{equation} that can be easily deduced from the definition \eqref{defmajorantr .}

elements.sent-2411-774: For any $\gamma_2 \geq \gamma_1\geq 0$, there exists a constant $C>0$ such that for all $n\geq1$ and $\bfh \in \ell^1_{\gamma_2 (\Z;\R)$, one has: \begin{equation}\left\| \left( S_{r,2}(n,j,\bfh) \right)_{j\in\Z} \right\|_{\ell^1_{\gamma_1}} + \left\| \left( S_{\ell,2}(n,j,\bfh) \right)_{j\in\Z} \right\|_{\ell^1_{\gamma_1}} \, \le \, \dfrac{C}{n^{\gamma_2+1/3}} \, \| \bfh \|_{\ell^1_{\gamma_2}} \, ,\end{equation} and therefore: \begin{equation}\left\| \left( S_{r,2}(n,j,\bfh) \right)_{j\in\Z} \right\|_{\ell^\infty_{\gamma_1}} + \left\| \left( S_{\ell,2}(n,j,\bfh) \right)_{j\in\Z} \right\|_{\ell^\infty_{\gamma_1}} \, \le \, \dfrac{C}{n^{\gamma_2+1/3}} \, \| \bfh \|_{\ell^1_{\gamma_2}} \, .\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-775: For $\bfh \in \ell^1_{\gamma_2 (\Z;\R)$, we have that \begin{equation}\left\| \left( S_{r,2}(n,j,\bfh) \right)_{j\in\Z} \right\|_{\ell^1_{\gamma_1}} \, = \, \left( \sum_{j\in\Z} \, (1+|j|^{\gamma_1}) \, \rme^{-c \, |j|} \right) \, \left( \sum_{j_0\geq1} M_r \left( c,-j_0+n \, |\alpha_r|,n \right) \, |h_{j_0}| \right) \le \, C \, \sum_{j_0=1}^{\frac{n \, |\alpha_r|}{2}} \, M_r \left( c,-j_0+n \, |\alpha_r|,n \right) \, |h_{j_0}| \, + \, C \, \sum_{j_0\geq\frac{n \, |\alpha_r|}{2}} \, M_r \left( c,-j_0+n \, |\alpha_r|,n \right) \, |h_{j_0}| \, .\end{equation}.}

elements.sent-2411-779: We now handle the most delicate estimates on the family of operators $\left(\cL^n_{\mathrm{act }\right)_{n\in\N^*}$.}
elements.sent-2411-780: For any $\gamma_2 \geq \gamma_1 \geq 0$, there exists a constant $C>0$ such that the family of operators $\left(\cL^n_{\mathrm{act }\right)_{n\in\N^*}$ satisfies the following estimate: \bqs \forall n \in\N^*\,,\quad \left\|\cL^n_{\mathrm{act \, \bfh \right\|_{\ell^1_{\gamma_1 \, \le \, \dfrac{C {n^{\gamma_2 \, \|\bfh\|_{\ell^1_{\gamma_2 \, ,\quad \text{ for any \bfh \in \ell^1_{\gamma_2 (\Z;\R) \text{ with \sum_{j\in\Z h_j=0 \, , \eqs and therefore: \bqs \forall n \in\N^*\, ,\quad \left\|\cL^n_{\mathrm{act \, \bfh \right\|_{\ell^\infty_{\gamma_1 \, \le \, \dfrac{C {n^{\gamma_2 \, \|\bfh\|_{\ell^1_{\gamma_2 \, ,\quad \text{ for any \bfh \in \ell^1_{\gamma_2 (\Z;\R) \text{ with \sum_{j\in\Z h_j=0 \, .. \eqs.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-782: The first step of the proof consists in writing: \begin{equation}\sum_{j_0\geq1}\mathds{1}_{|j-j_0|\leq n} A_r(-j_0+n \, |\alpha_r|,n) \, h_{j_0} +\sum_{j_0\leq0}\mathds{1}_{|j-j_0|\leq n}A_\ell(j_0+n \, \alpha_\ell,n) \, h_{j_0} \notag = \sum_{j_0\geq1}A_r(-j_0+n \, |\alpha_r|,n) \, h_{j_0} +\sum_{j_0\leq0}A_\ell(j_0+n \, \alpha_\ell,n) \, h_{j_0} \label{estim-activation-1} -\sum_{j_0\geq1}\mathds{1}_{|j-j_0|> n}A_r(-j_0+n \, |\alpha_r|,n) \, h_{j_0} -\sum_{j_0\leq0}\mathds{1}_{|j-j_0|> n}A_\ell(j_0+n \, \alpha_\ell,n) \, h_{j_0} \, ,\notag\end{equation} where the two infinite sums on the right-hand side converge since $\mathbf{A _r(-j_0+n \, |\alpha_r|,n)$ is uniformly bounded (see Corollary \ref{coro-A6 in Appendix \ref{appendixA ) and $\bfh$ is integrable.}}}

elements.sent-2411-785: In the first sum, since $n<|j-j_0|$ and $1\leq j_0\leq n \beta$, we get that $(1-\beta)n < |j|$, and thus \begin{equation}\sum_{j_0=1}^{n\beta}\mathds{1}_{|j-j_0|> n} \left|A_r(-j_0+n \, |\alpha_r|,n)\right| \, \left|h_{j_0}\right| \, \rme^{-c \, |j|} \le \sum_{j_0=1}^{n\beta}\mathds{1}_{|j-j_0|> n}\left|A_r(-j_0+n \, |\alpha_r|,n)\right| \, \left|h_{j_0}\right| \, \rme^{-\frac{c}{2}|j|}\rme^{-\frac{c(1-\beta)}{2}n} \leq C \, n \, \rme^{-\frac{c}{2}(1-\beta)n} \, \rme^{-\frac{c}{2}|j|} \, \| \bfh\|_{\ell^\infty(\Z)} \, ,\end{equation} where we have used the property from Corollary \ref{coro-A6 that the quantity $\left|\mathbf{A _r(-j_0+n \, |\alpha_r|,n)\right|$ is uniformly bounded and we have also used the fact that the sum with respect to $j_0$ gathers at most $n$ terms (since $\beta \ge 1$).}}

elements.sent-2411-800: Restricting to $n \ge 1$ (the case $n=0$ is trivial) and using the estimates obtained on the derivative of the temporal Green's function in Theorem~\ref{thmGreenDeriv , we obtain that \begin{equation}\left|\left(\cL^n(Id-S) \bfh\right)_j\right| \leq C \sum_{j_0\geq1}\mathds{1}_{|j-j_0|\leq n+1} \, \left[\mathds{1}_{j\geq1} K_r (c,j-j_0+n \, |\alpha_r|,n)+ \rme^{-c \, |j|} \, M_r (c,-j_0+|\alpha_r|n,n) \right] \, \left|h_{j_0}\right| ~~~+C \sum_{j_0\leq0} \mathds{1}_{|j-j_0|\leq n+1} \left[\mathds{1}_{j\leq0} \, K_\ell (c,j_0-j+n \, \alpha_\ell,n) +\rme^{-c \, |j|} \, M_\ell (c,j_0+n \, \alpha_\ell,n) \right] \, \left| h_{j_0} \right| ~~~+C \, \rme^{-c \, n} \, \sum_{j_0\in\Z} \,\mathds{1}_{|j-j_0|\leq n+1} \, \rme^{-c \, |j-j_0|} \, \left| h_{j_0} \right| \, .\end{equation}.}

elements.sent-2411-801: This motivates the definition of the following quantities for $(n,j) \in \N^*\times\Z$: \begin{equation}D_{r,1}(n,j,\bfh) \, := \, \mathds{1}_{j\geq1} \, \sum_{j_0\geq1} \, \mathds{1}_{|j-j_0|\leq n+1} \, K_r (c,j-j_0+n \, |\alpha_r|,n) \, \left| h_{j_0} \right| \, , D_{\ell,1}(n,j,\bfh) \, := \, \mathds{1}_{j\leq0} \, \sum_{j_0\leq0} \, \mathds{1}_{|j-j_0|\leq n+1} \, K_\ell (c,j_0-j+n \, \alpha_\ell,n) \, \left| h_{j_0} \right| \, ,\end{equation} and \begin{equation}D_{r,2}(n,j,\bfh) \, := \, \rme^{-c \, |j|} \, \sum_{j_0\geq1} \, M_r (c,-j_0+n \, |\alpha_r|,n) \, \left| h_{j_0} \right| \, , D_{\ell,2}(n,j,\bfh) \, := \, \rme^{-c \, |j|} \, \sum_{j_0 \leq 0} \, M_\ell (c,j_0+n \, \alpha_\ell,n) \, \left| h_{j_0} \right|\, ,\end{equation} together with: \bqs \mathfrak{D _{\mathrm{exp (n,j,\bfh) \, := \, \rme^{-c \, n \, \sum_{j_0\in\Z \, \mathds{1 _{|j-j_0|\leq n+1 \, \rme^{-c|j-j_0| \, \left| h_{j_0 \right| \, .}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-819: The first two terms contribute to \begin{equation}\sup_{j\in\Z}(1+|j|)^{\gamma_1} \mathds{1}_{j\geq1} \sum_{j_0=1}^j\mathds{1}_{|j-j_0|\leq n+1}K_r(c,j-j_0+n \, |\alpha_r|,n)|h_{j_0}| +\sup_{j\in\Z}(1+|j|)^{\gamma_1} \mathds{1}_{j\geq1} \sum_{j_0\geq j+1}\mathds{1}_{j_0<\frac{n \, |\alpha_r|}{2}}\mathds{1}_{|j-j_0|\leq n+1}K_r(c,j-j_0+n \, |\alpha_r|,n)|h_{j_0}| \leq C \rme^{-c \, n} \|\bfh\|_{\ell^\infty_{\gamma_2}},\end{equation} while for the third term, we note that for each $j\geq1$ \begin{equation}(1+|j|)^{\gamma_1} \sum_{j_0\geq j+1}\mathds{1}_{j_0\geq\frac{n \, |\alpha_r|}{2}}\mathds{1}_{|j-j_0|\leq n+1} K_r(c,j-j_0+n \, |\alpha_r|,n)|h_{j_0}| \leq C \sum_{j_0\geq1}\mathds{1}_{j_0\geq\frac{n \, |\alpha_r|}{2}}|\mathscr{K}_r^n(c,j-j_0)| \frac{(1+|j_0|)^{\gamma_2}}{(1+|j_0|)^{\gamma_2-\gamma_1}}|h_{j_0}| \leq \frac{C}{n^{\gamma_2-\gamma_1+1/8}}\|\bfh\|_{\ell^\infty_{\gamma_2}}\,,\end{equation} since $\|\left(\mathbf{K _r(j+n \, |\alpha_r|,n)\right)_{j\in\Z}\|_{\ell^1(\Z)}\leq C/n^{1/8}$.}
